_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 3. Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi.

cards.body: There's one word in Tagalog you can start using right now. Even in English sentences. That word is po.
Po makes any sentence respectful. There's no English equivalent.

example.note: Po works even in full English. Filipinos do this all the time.

cards.body: Add po to your greetings and they become respectful.

example.note: Use this with anyone older or in authority.

cards.source_text: You're greeting your boss in the morning.
cards.explanation: Magandang umaga po! Po goes at the end or after the key phrase.

cards.body: Naman is a softener. It makes things sound friendlier and more natural.

example.note: Naman here means 'also' or 'on my part.' It softens the reply.

cards.prompt: Which reply sounds the most natural?
cards.explanation: Mabuti naman, ikaw? Naman softens it and makes it conversational.

cards.body: Sige means 'okay,' 'sure,' or 'go ahead.' One of the most versatile words in Tagalog.

example.note: Sige po = okay/sure (respectful). You'll use this constantly.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Sige comes from the Spanish 'sigue' (continue/go on). Filipinos use it for everything: agreeing, saying goodbye, even ending phone calls.

cards.source_text: Your teacher says: See you tomorrow.
cards.explanation: Sige po = okay (respectful). Perfect for responding to someone in authority.

cards.prompt: Your grandmother asks how you are. Reply respectfully and ask her back.
cards.explanation: Mabuti naman po, ikaw po? = I'm good, and you? (respectful)

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: Po has no direct English translation. It changes the entire tone of a sentence without changing its meaning.
Kids are taught to say po from the moment they can speak. It's one of the first social rules Filipino children learn.
Forgetting po with an elder isn't like cursing. It's more like forgetting to say 'please.' People notice, but they won't get angry.
